card.indications: Major depressive disorder in adults and generalized anxiety disorder in adults; pediatric indications depend on age and current labeling.
card.mechanism: Selectively inhibits serotonin reuptake in the central nervous system, increasing serotonergic activity.
card.dosing: Adults commonly start at 10 mg once daily; may increase to 20 mg once daily after at least 1 week when clinically appropriate. Lower or slower dosing may be needed in older adults or hepatic impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Give once daily, morning or evening, with or without food. Taper gradually rather than stopping abruptly.
card.contraindications: Concurrent or recent MAO inhibitor use, pimozide, or hypersensitivity. Avoid starting within the required washout period after an MAOI.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for increased suicidal thoughts and behavior in children, adolescents, and young adults. Risks include serotonin syndrome, QT prolongation, hyponatremia, abnormal bleeding, mania activation, and discontinuation symptoms.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, insomnia, fatigue, somnolence, sweating, decreased libido, delayed ejaculation, and dry mouth.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Suicidal behavior, serotonin syndrome, severe hyponatremia, seizures, mania, major bleeding, and serious allergic reaction.
card.interactions: MAO inhibitors and other serotonergic drugs increase serotonin-syndrome risk. NSAIDs, aspirin, anticoagulants, and antiplatelets increase bleeding. Other QT-prolonging drugs require caution.
card.monitoring: Monitor mood, suicide risk, anxiety, sleep, sodium in at-risk patients, bleeding, serotonin-syndrome symptoms, and ECG when clinically indicated.
card.patientTeaching: Benefits may take several weeks. Do not stop suddenly. Report suicidal thoughts, agitation, fever, tremor, severe diarrhea, confusion, unusual bleeding, or severe headache.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid alcohol. Maintain hydration, especially if nausea occurs.

card.indications: GERD, erosive esophagitis, risk reduction of NSAID-associated gastric ulcers, H. pylori regimens, and pathologic hypersecretory conditions depending on formulation.
card.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits the gastric parietal-cell hydrogen-potassium ATPase, suppressing basal and stimulated acid secretion.
card.dosing: Common adult GERD dosing is 20 to 40 mg once daily before meals; dose and duration vary by indication. Reduce or individualize prolonged high-dose therapy when clinically appropriate.
card.routeAdministration: Give at least 1 hour before meals. Swallow delayed-release capsules whole or open and sprinkle on approved soft food without crushing granules. IV products require proper reconstitution and infusion.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to substituted benzimidazoles or product components. Concomitant rilpivirine-containing products are contraindicated.
card.warnings: Long-term or high-dose use may increase risk of C. difficile diarrhea, fractures, hypomagnesemia, vitamin B12 deficiency, interstitial nephritis, fundic-gland polyps, and lupus-like reactions.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, constipation, and flatulence.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute interstitial nephritis, severe hypomagnesemia with arrhythmia or seizures, C. difficile colitis, severe skin reactions, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: May reduce absorption of drugs requiring an acidic pH. Can reduce clopidogrel activation and increase exposure to some CYP2C19 substrates. Methotrexate levels may rise at high doses.
card.monitoring: Monitor symptom relief, GI bleeding, renal function if symptoms suggest nephritis, magnesium during prolonged therapy, and B12 or bone health in long-term high-risk use.
card.patientTeaching: Take before meals and do not crush delayed-release granules. Report persistent diarrhea, reduced urine, rash, muscle cramps, palpitations, or black stools.
card.nutrition: Take before food. Limit personal trigger foods, late meals, alcohol, and tobacco when treating reflux.

card.indications: Adjunct to diet for primary hyperlipidemia and selected inherited lipid disorders, alone or with statin therapy.
card.mechanism: Inhibits intestinal NPC1L1-mediated absorption of cholesterol and plant sterols, reducing delivery of cholesterol to the liver and lowering LDL cholesterol.
card.dosing: 10 mg orally once daily, with or without food. No routine dose titration is required.
card.routeAdministration: Give once daily. When used with a bile-acid sequestrant, give ezetimibe at least 2 hours before or 4 hours after the sequestrant.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity. When combined with a statin, apply that statin's contraindications, including active liver disease or pregnancy where applicable.
card.warnings: Can cause elevated liver enzymes and myopathy, especially with statins or fibrates. Rare rhabdomyolysis and hypersensitivity reactions occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Diarrhea, arthralgia, fatigue, upper respiratory symptoms, and abdominal discomfort.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, hepatitis, marked transaminase elevation, anaphylaxis, and angioedema.
card.interactions: Statins and fibrates increase muscle and liver risk. Cyclosporine can increase ezetimibe exposure. Bile-acid sequestrants reduce absorption unless doses are separated.
card.monitoring: Monitor lipid panel, liver enzymes when combined with a statin or if symptoms occur, and CK when muscle pain or weakness is significant.
card.patientTeaching: Continue diet and exercise measures. Report unexplained muscle pain, dark urine, jaundice, or severe weakness.
card.nutrition: Follow a heart-healthy diet low in saturated fat and cholesterol. May be taken with or without food.

card.indications: Duodenal and gastric ulcers, GERD, erosive esophagitis, and pathologic hypersecretory conditions; OTC products treat or prevent heartburn.
card.mechanism: Blocks histamine H2 receptors on gastric parietal cells, reducing gastric acid and pepsin secretion.
card.dosing: Common adult prescription dosing ranges from 20 mg twice daily to 40 mg once daily depending on indication. Dose reduction is required in moderate to severe renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: May be given with or without food. Bedtime dosing is common for ulcer therapy. IV doses should be administered at the recommended dilution and rate.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to famotidine or other H2 blockers. Use caution in renal impairment and older adults.
card.warnings: CNS effects such as confusion, delirium, and hallucinations may occur, especially with renal impairment or advanced age. QT prolongation is possible with accumulation.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Headache, dizziness, constipation, diarrhea, and abdominal discomfort.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe confusion, arrhythmia, blood dyscrasias, interstitial nephritis, hepatitis, anaphylaxis, and severe skin reactions.
card.interactions: May reduce absorption of drugs requiring gastric acidity. Renal impairment increases accumulation. Few CYP-mediated interactions compared with cimetidine.
card.monitoring: Monitor symptom relief, GI bleeding, renal function, mental status, and persistent or recurrent symptoms that require evaluation.
card.patientTeaching: Take only for the recommended duration. Report black stools, vomiting blood, persistent pain, confusion, rash, or palpitations.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Avoid foods, alcohol, caffeine, or tobacco that worsen reflux or ulcer symptoms.

card.indications: Severe pain requiring potent opioid therapy. Transdermal fentanyl is for opioid-tolerant patients with persistent pain; transmucosal products are generally for breakthrough cancer pain in opioid-tolerant patients.
card.mechanism: Potent mu-opioid receptor agonist that alters pain perception and response while depressing respiratory drive and GI motility.
card.dosing: Highly formulation specific and based on opioid tolerance, prior opioid exposure, age, organ function, and response. Never convert or substitute formulations without an approved conversion method.
card.routeAdministration: Use continuous respiratory and sedation monitoring for IV therapy. Apply patches to intact skin, remove old patches, avoid heat, and never cut patches. Handle transmucosal products exactly as labeled.
card.contraindications: Significant respiratory depression, acute or severe asthma without monitoring, known or suspected GI obstruction, and use of transdermal or transmucosal products in opioid-naive patients.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include addiction, abuse, misuse, life-threatening respiratory depression, accidental exposure, medication errors, neonatal withdrawal, and profound sedation or death with benzodiazepines or other CNS depressants. Heat can cause fatal patch overdose.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Sedation, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, constipation, sweating, itching, and confusion.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Fatal respiratory depression, chest-wall rigidity with rapid IV dosing, severe hypotension, coma, serotonin syndrome, adrenal insufficiency, overdose, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: Benzodiazepines, alcohol, gabapentinoids, sedating antihistamines, and other CNS depressants increase respiratory risk. CYP3A4 inhibitors may markedly increase exposure; inducers may reduce effect or trigger withdrawal.
card.monitoring: Monitor respiratory rate, oxygenation, sedation, pain, blood pressure, bowel function, misuse risk, patch placement, and naloxone availability.
card.patientTeaching: Use only as prescribed, avoid alcohol and sedatives, store locked, and dispose safely. Keep patches away from children and heat sources. Seek emergency help for slow breathing or inability to awaken.
card.nutrition: Maintain fluids and fiber unless restricted. Avoid alcohol and grapefruit when advised because of interaction risk.
